text.title: Diary Study Analysis
text.summary: Codebook design, coding pass rules, thematic and temporal analysis, quantitative metrics, and report structure for diary study data.

rule.statement: Lock the codebook before coding begins. Adding a theme mid-study invalidates earlier passes — re-run the coder agent on the full dataset if the codebook changes.
rule.rationale: Cross-participant frequency counts are only valid when all entries are coded against the same taxonomy. Mid-study codebook additions create systematic gaps in earlier participant data.

rule.statement: Keep the codebook to 6-12 themes with explicit definitions. Force the coder to return an empty themes array when no code fits rather than inventing a match.
rule.rationale: Larger codebooks produce lower inter-rater reliability. Agents over-classify when forced to assign a code to every entry — a NULL result is real data about entry quality or scope drift.

rule.statement: Code each entry independently without exposing the coder to other participants' entries or prior hypotheses. Run two independent passes on a 10% sample and compute Cohen's kappa. Below 0.6 means the codebook is ambiguous.
rule.rationale: Hypothesis exposure causes confirmation bias that amplifies in agents when the prompt mentions a research question upfront. Kappa below 0.6 indicates the codebook has ambiguous boundaries that will produce unreliable frequency counts.

rule.statement: Require the coder to cite verbatim text fragments per theme. This lets the synthesizer pull real quotes without re-reading raw entries.
rule.rationale: Paraphrased quotes introduce researcher interpretation into participant voice. Verbatim extraction is the only defensible standard for evidence in a research report.

rule.statement: Tag every entry with its study day and prompt type (signal-contingent vs event-contingent vs end-of-day). Compute per-day sentiment timelines with date metadata — not free-text dates from entry content.
rule.rationale: Temporal patterns (e.g., day 3-5 frustration spike) are invisible to a single-pass summarizer. Free-text date parsing is unreliable; structured metadata is required for timeline visualizations.

list.item: Temporal patterns: behavior changes over time, learning curves, habit formation points, drop-off triggers.
Contextual patterns: location-based behaviors, device preferences, time-of-day patterns.
Thematic patterns: recurring problems, common delighters, unmet needs, workarounds.

section.title: Report Structure

rule.statement: Run rolling synthesis from day 3, not batch at study end. Late synthesis loses the chance to add follow-up probes while participants are still active.
rule.rationale: Diary entries become the interview script for exit interviews. Themes identified early can generate targeted follow-up questions. Waiting until end produces a comprehensive analysis of an unprobed dataset.

section.reference: Analysis synthesis skeleton: participation summary, theme blocks with verbatim quotes, temporal patterns, recommendations.

list.item: Executive summary: key findings (3-5 bullets) and top recommendations.
Method: study design, participant profile, data collection details.
Findings: themes with evidence, temporal patterns, context patterns, quantitative summary.
Recommendations: prioritized actions with evidence and success metrics.
Appendix: full data, additional quotes, methodology details including bias mitigations.
